speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 10, 1995. My name is Gloria [? Promen ?] Schneider, and we are interviewing Matthew Guttman in Queens, New York. This is Tape 2. Mr. Guttman, you were talking about being in Briansk, and that the Russians--

speaker: interviewee
text: --running. They pushed us, and we stopped. Sometimes, we stopped. We had to dig the same trenches all over again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were carrying ammunition. We were doing lots of kinds of things, lots of kinds of work. And, of course, we were freezing. But we tried-- we went into the farmers, Russian farmers. They didn't even have themselves clothing, but we were changing, because they were suffering also. Since the Germans inundated their territory, they were suffering from lots of things-- for instance, thread or soap. And we had, somehow, because they gave us soap to wash ourselves. Thread, needles, they didn't have. And got some kind of clothes-- you know, their own, what they were maybe junking them. But at least they kept you a little bit warm. Until-- we were running like that until 1944 in the spring. We arrived into former Poland. Now is Ukraine, there. We came through [? Chamis, ?] [INAUDIBLE], Sambir, Staryi Sambir, Turka, into-- through the Carpathian Mountains into Czechoslovakia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But in between, we were working. We were still working, but from our company-- not only it was decimated, they got lost by the running. They got lost, and they got-- maybe we were, from 224 people, we remained 17 from the original company.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you keep your strength up?

speaker: interviewee
text: My strength up? You were forced to. There were a lots-- there were some people, they remained behind. They should be captured by the Russians, and then they're going to be finished. I did not have-- not I had opportunity, but I didn't want to. Because my father was a prisoner in the First World War in Russia. And he told me, 'Don't you ever-- if they are going to take you into the battle line, and you are going to come to Russia on the eastern front, don't you ever try to go through in prison. Because they didn't let me, only two years later, after the war, to come home. They keep you, and they are working with you, and they are taking you to Siberia, and so on and so on,' he says. And I kept that in my mind, my father's teaching, and I didn't go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was running with the other ones. I tried to make myself useful. I didn't complain. It was times when we really were very, very hungry. And the-- the army, they couldn't bring food for them, and for us, neither. But they'd rather feed them than us. We were not so valuable than the-- the soldier who was fighting the war. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were digging out the potatoes where the farmer plant potatoes into the ground. We were digging them out, and we were eating them. The farmer couldn't say a word, because they went, and they took away their cattle. They took away everything else. So they were afraid to say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, we came through into former Czechoslovakia. And then really, we started running. Because then they were pushing us, and pushing us even further.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was all summer long. We were working here a little bit, working there. Some people ran away. I had a friend of mine in Budapest, where I used to live, my landlady's brother I met over there, in the Ukraine. And we kept ourselves together until the Russians were behind us about 15 kilometers. And it was night, and we were so damn tired, so we were walking back.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said-- he said to me-- he says, 'You know what, Matthew? Let's go into the straw,' he says, 'and hide in the straw. Tomorrow, the Russians are going to be here, and we are going to be liberated.' I says, 'What happens if the Hungarians or the Germans capture you? You know you are going to be shot on the place.' He says, 'Don't be afraid.'

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what had happened. He went, and I-- whenever it was an alarm-- 4 o'clock in the morning, it was our alarm, the Russians starting to push. It was an alarm, the [INAUDIBLE]. I stayed-- put myself on the line with the other ones. And we went here, there. German soldiers, they went with the-- with the bayonets and their guns. And they started into the straw, and they were hollering. He says, 'Don't-- don't shoot! Don't shoot! We are here.' They took out about 25 people. They killed them on the place there, with my landlady's brother, together. [SIGHS] Finally, they took us so long, the Hungarians-- they took us so long, now, until they took us to a place by the Austrian border, Austrian-Hungarian border. That was in Hungary, that was called [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There, Christmas Day, they gave us over to the Gestapo. They didn't have what to do no more with us. Their country was already invaded from the Russians. They didn't have where to take us no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then, they surrendered us. They gave it-- gave us over to the Gestapo. Now, here, start the [NON-ENGLISH]. Did you know what to expect? Did you know what the Germans were doing to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know that that is waiting for us, because I would have remained behind, too. I'd rather go in the Russian prison camp and come home later than never. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This [NON-ENGLISH] was a big sugar factory, and that, what they made a concentration camp from-- from that. It was no more factory, no more sugar factory. But the railway tracks were there. The dirt carloads were there-- I mean, the empty carloads were there. We arrived there at night, and they count us. The Hungarian captain gave it-- gave us over to the Gestapo. The Gestapo didn't even understood what the captain says, because he was talking Hungarian. This was German.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he took over the Jews. But we were [? demonstrated, ?] you know, like soldiers. '1, 2, 3, 4,' you know? We were going, you know, singing, you know, on the street, you know. Because instead of guns, we had the utilities-- I mean, the-- the shovels, and the axes, and so on, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here, there were some old people brought from Budapest. And I saw, when we were staying in the line, that they were bringing out the-- the soup. It was the night-- dinner, a soup. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there were the old people. They were staying in the line with a dish, and the guy was giving them about a little soup. And he was begging him. He says, [INAUDIBLE], do me a favor, [INAUDIBLE]. Friend, give me one more, a little bit more.' The Gestapo came with a stick and started hitting him over the head, and over the head, and over the head, until he spilled his soup. And he put, you know, his dish on the head. And this [? shouldn't ?] kill him. And he ran away, without nothing. I saw what is. In the morning, they put us on some barracks. In the morning, we woke up-- [GERMAN]! 'Out, out!' They are going, 'Out, [GERMAN] appel.' [GERMAN] appel meant the counting. You had to stay in the line, five in a row. And this is the way they are going to count them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- you should freeze to death, because it was Christmas. It was cold, horribly cold, punishable cold. They took pails of cold water, and they was just shooting over your head, you know. You should freeze to death. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a very nice, sunny day. It was at Christmas Day, December the 25th, 1944. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The-- the head Gestapo is coming out, and the other Gestapo-- he says, 'Hansel!' [? 'Ja.' ?] On the railway tracks, there were frozen, naked bodies-- but skeletons, of course. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: He starts-- the head Gestapo starts laughing like a horse. 'Ha ha, ha ha, ha ha!' 'Hansel, [GERMAN].' 'Hansel, what are you doing here? Are you making here a beach? They are tanning themselves on the sun on the beach [INAUDIBLE].' When I saw that, I said to myself, 'How long is it going to take until I am going to look the same?'

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Here, they took us out to work. One day, they brought in-- what-- we were digging trenches. Really, they didn't need the trenches, because here, they didn't have a chance that they should be against Russia. Because Russia was pushing when they were pushing. They didn't [? look ?] at nothing. Everything what had-- it had a goal. They were dying, but they were pushing. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were digging trenches in-- on the field. Once, they took us to a corn field. The corn field was frozen dead, it was-- it was snow over it-- and digging trenches. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [? Lucky ?] we were getting here food. We were getting beans and horse meat. It was good. You won't believe. We were getting beans and horse meat. But everyday, the same thing. And we were getting the diarrhea, dysentery. We were digging the trenches in the corn field, and it looks like the snow fell too soon. They didn't have the time to pick the corn. And we came on top of the corn, and we were digging for the corn. And we made a little fire, there in the trenches. They allowed us to make a little fire in the trenches. The young juveniles, the Hungarian young juveniles like the SS, the Hitlerites-- they put them they should take care of us, because a soldier had to fight. Here, 17, 18-year-old kids with guns. [SIGHS] I-- kept-- [? I'm ?] getting from one place into the other. They brought in a big transport over from Budapest-- old people, rich people, fur coats, and the foxes, whatever. Very elegant people, with truckloads of people. They brought them in, and they were unorganized, undisciplined. And, I think, at night, when we came, we came like soldiers. You know, through the gate, and they gave them, you know, the reports. 'So many, so many I have here. And one died, or one was wounded,' and so the whole-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They was looking around, that they, the old people, they can't do nothing at all. One afternoon, they grabbed them into truckloads and took them out in a field, and they shot them all up. When we came in at night, we saw them. All those elegant people with their fur coats, all butchered up.

speaker: interviewer
text: We had to unload them from the truck, to undress them, to dig big ditches, and to lay them down. One with the head that way, and the other one with the head this way. How many more they should be getting?

speaker: interviewee
text: The clothes-- they had to undress them completely, because the clothes could say witness that who you are. But this way, they were pouring some kind of chemical over the bodies, that they should rot. And they should burn sooner, rot faster, you know-- they rot in the ground. Now, we were working in the field. At night, I came-- it was a big pile of clothes outside. They let them rot there, the clothes-- fur coats, and foxes, and whatever. They let them rot outside. They didn't-- didn't have use for them. I used to bring in corn. We used to-- I used to burn the corn on the field there, by the fire. And I filled up, under my shirt, between my shirt and my body with corn ears, burned up. That was our medicine, I guess, the dysentery. If you eat it, it pulled together your stomach, your loose stomach. And people used to give away their daily ration of bread for an ear-- a corn of-- an ear corn. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the people in the camp were all Jewish? The people who were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jewish. Once they brought in in these-- one of these transports, they brought in a young boy. He was about 17 years old-- blonde, blue eyes. And he was crying in Hungarian. He was crying out loud, 'They made a mistake with me! They made a mistake with me!' And he had a big cross on his-- here, on his breast. 'They made a mistake with me! I am Catholic!' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was crying, you know. He saw what is going on. He saw his future. He says, 'They made a mistake.'

speaker: interviewer
text: I says, 'What kind of mistake did they made with you?' He says, 'I am Catholic. My father is Catholic.' 'How about your grandfather?' He says, 'My grandfather was-- is-- he went over from Jew, became Catholic. He should marry my mother, my grandmother.'

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, 'You are the third generation, and you are a Jew. They didn't made no nothing. They didn't made no mistake with you.' But he was crying all night long. He was crying that loud that during the night, he died. He died. He-- he couldn't sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know. Did he help him to die? Because we-- we couldn't sleep. His cryings and his loud hollerings. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe somebody did him a favor.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing, I had-- my sub-commandant was a Jew, a former captain in the Hungarian army. But now, he was no more a captain, he was a Jew. He, as soon as we arrived, by the Gestapo, he was looking on the wall. For two days in a row, day and night, he didn't do nothing. Dressed like that, he looked. And he was a very fine-- he was a professor, a violin professor. I remember everywhere, France, you know. He looked at the wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to him, 'Hey, George.' [? Klein ?] was his name. I said to him, 'George, what are you seeing there?'

speaker: interviewee
text: He was looking only on the bare wall, nothing doing. He was turned to the wall, was looking. That means that he was-- he gave up. He died. He didn't-- Did nobody helped him to die, but he died. He didn't eat, he didn't drink. He was just looking on the wall. Anyway, I-- at night, I came in. I thought to myself, those furs are staying there, and I am over here. The most thing what I would like to have is at least a vest to keep me warm and making gloves. There were Persian coats, Persian lamb coats. I started, you know, with a knife, sharpening the knife on a rock. And I had thread-- I had, I had needle and thread. And one [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hand glove, I made.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those young Nazis, those Hungarian Nazis, where they were taking care of us by the work there, says to me-- says, 'Where do you have that glove from?' Because they were freezing too, of course. I said, 'I made them.' He says, 'Can you make me one, a pair?' He says, 'I am-- my hands are freezing, too.' I said, 'Sure.'

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, 'I'm going to give you bread.' I says, 'OK, I am already in business.' And I started making gloves. And I thought that this-- particularly this one guy, I was doing business with him, because he was taking gloves, you know, to his aunt, and to his uncle, and to his father and mother. And he used to bring me bread. At least I used to bring to some friends of mine, the clothes surrounding. We used to have something what to eat, boiled eggs. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But during the work, we are in the trenches, and it was cold. You were allowed to go, you know, five minutes to warm up to the fire. And it was also in the trenches, made the fire. And then back to work, and the other one should come, and so on and so on. And this particular guy, I was friendly with him. I mean, friendly how much it goes. He-- he says to one guy-- he says, 'Hey.' We were smiling at him, because he was nice. We used to call him 'Mr. Commandant,' because you give him, you know, a title. You couldn't say to him, 'Hey, you!' 'Mr. Commandant.'

speaker: interviewer
text: He says to the guy, because he was smiling-- he says to him-- he says, 'What are you smiling? What are you happy about?'

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, 'I am happy that my mother made me.' You know, just like, [? it's ?] a joke, [INAUDIBLE] not smiling. And he was smiling, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, 'What would you say if I would kill you?' He says, 'Nothing.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He says, 'OK, take off your hat.' It is act! Everything goes-- we think that it is a joke.

speaker: interviewee
text: This friend of mine takes off his hat. Pow! He shot him in the head. He says-- now he says, 'Bury him.' Just like that, just that much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were there in Mauthausen, yes. In the meantime, I-- But this is in-- you're still in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nagycenk. In the meantime, I got sick-- typhoid fever, there. And from the regular barrack, they had orders they should take you-- when you were diseased, they should take you into the diseased barrack. It was a barrack. Nobody took care of you. Nobody gave you nothing. I had a friend of mine-- he died there. He used to come into the barrack. He used to bring me dinner every night. He used to bring me my ration bread. [SIGHS] But I was already so sick. I was burning up in fever. I didn't know already about myself, but he was still to throw the bread, mopped my head. And I was, you know, like-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't know about myself. That was going on for about maybe a week. The dysentery-- it was going out of you, only [? pus. ?] I didn't have no time. Because it was, by the door, it was no bathroom, or anything like that, or latrines. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By the door, it was a big pile of straw. And the people who had the time to run until there-- they made it in the straw, and that's all. But nobody cleaned it away. Everything was there. In the morning, they used to come and collect the dead bodies out on the railway tracks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, of course, they used to bury them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was here, the diseased barrack, about-- until I wake up. I wake up slowly. It took me about two days to come back from coma, like-- it was like-- I was, like in a coma. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Hungry like a dog. Somehow, I put my hands-- I'm looking around. My head is on something, like on a rock. I am-- putting my hands. And whatever I found there in the dark, I'm putting it in my mouth. And I see it's old bread, stale bread, what my friend throw it to my head. And I found the bread there. For about three or four days, I was eating the bread. I didn't have no time to run to the pile. I was letting it, everything, in my pants. I had no change or whatever. My body was getting wounded and rotten, you know, from the dirt. But slowly, but slowly, was getting spring, around March, and the sun was getting a little bit warmer. And I went, and I opened up the door. And somehow-- I went on all my fours, because I couldn't walk. I was so weak. I was shivering from my knee. And I was walking slowly on my fours to the door. And I opened up a crack in the door, the sun should shine on me. And slowly by slowly, everyday, it was a little bit better. And this friend of mine brought me, still, my dinners and my bread. And then, I notice, he fell-- he fell sick, diseased. But he was lucky, and he died. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And outside was a pile with the diseased people, what they were already so diseased that there was no hope for them. So they put them outside under the free sky. They gave them the blankets to cover themselves, but the frost was so big. And there were two friends, it looks like there were two friends. And one-- in the morning, I see that he puts his hand. Now, I started to walk around already. And slowly, I stole myself out from the diseased barrack, and I went into the healthy one. And it was in the morning, around 9 o'clock. One puts the hand-- those where they were laying outside under the free sky, covered with the blanket. His friend died during the night. And he put a hand on his head, and he gave him a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. He started, you know, the prayer after the death. He was singing it, you know, with his shivering voice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a Gestapo man passes by. He says, [GERMAN] 'Is he delirious? He's singing.' I said, no, he's not singing. That is the prayer of death.

speaker: interviewee
text: His friend died during the night. And so, he just left them there. So another friend of mine ran away, somehow, from work.
